sentences.text: Life before the war was pretty good.
The same-- there was a life.
They were-- they were-- they was working.
My father was work-- he was a tailor.
We helped out each other, sure.
My father was every Friday go out and sell them, sell outside.
He was a tailor for people, too.
They get him to do something.
Twice a week, we got the market.
Brothers, we got four, five, six-- six brothers.
He was a little boy, he died.
Oh, it was a long time ago.
Oh, yeah, we got a big field.
The second street from me, the kids go play ball-- soccer game.
Where a lot of people were there.
No, no, not with a courtyard.
No, they got separated, I got separated.
And after this I go to work.
I was working by, how they call them, the sweetness-- chocolate, yeah, cookies, a bakery, yeah.
After this, when the war come out, go away.
Come night for Passover, we got together.
The whole family get together, and that's all.
The grandparents was-- lived far away.
And after this, we go eat.
And they'd say, go have a good time.
When you baked things at the bakery, did you have special favorite things that you liked to bake?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother bake-- yeah, my mother baked lots-- lot of stuff.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Today nobody do nothing.
The American people no like the baking, right?
This time I was about 20 years.
Even a girl, was good to go out, too.
They were-- they come on in my home town.
They say, out, out, out, out, out out.
They, they was happy they come inside.
And after this, oh, we have to go.
After little later, they open up the concentration camp.
When they coming in, I move out.
I move out to the-- far away from them.
Me, my father and mother, the whole family go to a farm.
We've got customers, and they come to us.
And my father make a tailor for them, do everything for them.
They told-- they told us come over to-- with us for a while.
Like we'd go some place and start fighting, I tell them, why, I fix you up for good.
And I fix them up for good.
We take a couple the people.
We in a straight inside, and we make it for them.
We wait for him, all the guys.
We go after him, after the guys.
They arrested the guy, a couple guys, and we go home.
I got business with the police.
Even over here, I got business with the police.
They come to me, need something.
No, let me tell you this, there was all the kind Germans.
The good Germans, and the bad Germans.
Like over here, good people, bad people.
Nothing, they just come in, look around, everything, and that's all.
And they say, you have to go work.
I work by the German too.
Yeah, we go work with them.
After this, we go work off the airport.
No, this is after the farm.
After they go some place else, then we have to go back.
They called, the German comes over, they tell you you have to go to work.
But they tell me go to work, and I tell them to go back home.
My brothers and my sisters, they no was in Cracow.
Your sis-- my sisters and my brothers.
About six people, six, seven people.
In my home town, in the house.
When I worked, they put it in the camp.
No, no, no, see, they come to our house.
They tell them the Jewish organization, the committee, you have to go work.
They got an order, after they need the people.
And after this, we'll go to their-- the, the camps.
He would come of the people, tell them, hey, we need people.
And this time, was actually Mr.
He go to Plaszow, where I was.
Need to tell why you kill the people.
Me, my brother, my sister, they go someplace else.
And me with my brother, we go to Plaszow.
The parents were still that-- the parents, I think they took it someplace else.
Oh, there was a labor camp.
We get that work, we have to go work.
No, it was the SS-- the SS, the soldiers.
The SS, the soldiers, they no like the SS.
Sure, yeah, they was enemies with each other.
Well, the guys not so good like in the house.
A different kind of criminal, yeah.
Christian, Jews, all another one, religion.
And see, in Plaszow, there were different kind.
And now after this, they move-- they build a new building for the people.
And after this, there's-- when there was the war, an American plane fall down on the camp.
Fall down on our camp, then the SS looking at the-- all the people here.
They fall down, and then nothing happened.
They take away the two pilots with the plane.
They give them respect salute, and they take away them. yeah.
Who knows what's going to happen to them?
Look the German told me, look, Amerikaner.
I ask him, you like them?
Don't think the German like the Germans.
It was hard, terrible, ooh boy.
Take them out, put it in.
We both were together from first until finished.
And after this, Schindler had come-- he come.
Schindler had come, and had tell he wants some people.
You have to do it what they say.
When he-- we-- if he was going, I'll go with him.
What's happened like-- for Schindler, you see, Schindler come.
He go to the bus when it come from the camp.
He tell them, I want people.
And that way, a friend of mine, he called, come on, come on, come on.
He took me, and after, he took my brother.
A lot of people took from the camp.
Not [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] we go to the [INAUDIBLE], to them.
No, Krakow, we didn't make ammunition.
He'd-- he come over there, and he'd tell that he wants the people.
He wants the people get to work.
He'd tell ever-- come on let's go.
Go, go, go, go, go, go.
We need-- somebody looking for you.
And a friend of mine, told him to go on.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] See, when I wouldn't go to him, they'd be gone by a long time away.
Ah, a train for the-- for us, a person plane.
Like all the time, the planes.
After there, we go someplace else.
They give us the cattle planes.
From Plaszow to Gross Rosen, I thinks that we go two, two days.
We was in Gross Rosen, we stay all of overnight.
I don't want to tell nothing about what's going on.
Overnight, and on the morning, he comes and tell them, come on!
Come on, come on, come, on.
He took a man and a woman.
Saw, saw him in, in, in Czechoslovakia.
Over there, and I talked to him.
No, Gross Rosen, we just sleeping on the floor.
[INAUDIBLE] because come middle of the night, or at in the morning, I tell them, come on.
Come the other way, take out the people.
He sits over there night time, night.
They kill people in the oven, put on the bakery.
Sure, they're putting the people right in the bakery.
Then who was go to Schindler, he tell them, there in Gross Rosen don't touch the people.
Not the men, not the woman.
They're mine's, and I take back.
In the morning, he take everybody, everybody who belonged to him, and going.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Was supposed to talk about the situation was.
Was supposed to talk all night.
You have to go to home.
So what-- I say a lot of friends, Polacks, Polish people-- I ask, what are you doing over here?
You want to go with me?
No, I have to go home back.
There a lot of international people.
Was German even, Russian, all that kind.
What they living in their hometown someplace.
She went over there like that.
But they took a big shower-- from the shower, straight to the oven.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Young people, kids, everything.
And I wish to say, the young people, the young generation over here, to watch your steps, and be ready.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They think I left.
We see that look like now.
You see, Europe, what's going on again.
Better in a wish, another war comes up in Europe.
